evidence: Careerspan isn't a replacement for Handshake or any recruiting platform—it's the 'missing layer' that makes their products defensible. This reframes the entire partnership narrative from competition to symbiosis.
Distribution platforms (Handshake) have solved access, but access without signal is noise. Signal technology (Careerspan) completes the puzzle.

Handshake has 1,600 universities but no way to capture behavioral signal at scale. Careerspan has the coaching infrastructure to capture signal but lacks distribution. The combination creates a data moat neither could build alone.

Instead of trying to sell Careerspan as a product, you're selling the opportunity to build a proprietary dataset that competitors can't replicate. This isn't about revenue—it's about owning the future of talent identification.

Positioning Careerspan as a strategic hedge for Handshake's declining core business ($200M, only 6% growth). Even if the partnership never becomes a major revenue line, it provides data supremacy and option value.
Their core recruiting business essentially flat for last year. They're like moving away from that line of business.

Careerspan is playing a relationship-heavy, founder-to-founder game rather than a traditional enterprise sales motion. The 'who you know' is more valuable than 'what you know' in early-stage partnership development.

Both Vrijen and Logan are seeing surprising social media growth (20% and 2,000 followers respectively) with minimal posting. This suggests that educational content about careers and recruiting has organic demand.

Careerspan's positioning around 'elite talent identification' represents a strategic pivot from general career coaching. This moves them upstream in the value chain—from helping anyone improve, to helping organizations identify top performers.
The real money in recruiting isn't in volume—it's in high-value placements. Companies will pay premium for better identification of top 1% talent.
We're a three-person team. Like we're not even that expensive essentially to like to bring on. Emphasizing the 3-person team as an asset turns a potential weakness into strength.
In an era of bloated startups with high burn rates, being lean makes you an attractive acquisition target or partnership partner. We're not expensive to acquire, but we're strategically valuable.

The entire Careerspan thesis rests on the premise that behavioral signal (how people think) is more valuable than profile data (what they've done). This challenges the fundamental premise of LinkedIn and traditional resumes.

The emphasis on month-on-month retention and return rates reveals a long-term data play. The real value isn't in helping someone get one job—it's in tracking their entire career trajectory and predicting future success.

I would almost rather you highlight the Corridor X partnership... recruiters are already using career span to identify talent. The team isn't throwing everything at the wall—they're selecting metrics that align with the specific prospect's interests.
